flashcards.front: What is the Fundamental Theorem of Counting?
flashcards.back: If task A can be done in m ways and task B can be done in n ways, then both tasks can be done together in m × n ways. Extends to any finite number of sequential tasks.
The product of all positive integers from 1 to n: n! = n × (n-1) × (n-2) × ... × 2 × 1. Also defined: 0! = 1.
An arrangement of objects in a specific order. The number of permutations of n objects taken r at a time is P(n,r) = n!/(n-r)!.
A selection of objects without regard to order. The number of combinations of n objects taken r at a time is C(n,r) = n!/[r!(n-r)!] = n choose r.
If n items are placed into m containers and n > m, then at least one container must contain more than one item. Generalized: if n items go into m boxes, some box has at least ceil(n/m) items.

flashcards.front: What is the Inclusion-Exclusion Principle for two sets?
flashcards.back: For sets A and B: |A ∪ B| = |A| + |B| - |A ∩ B|. For three sets: |A ∪ B ∪ C| = |A|+|B|+|C| - |A∩B| - |A∩C| - |B∩C| + |A∩B∩C|.

flashcards.front: When do we use Permutations vs Combinations?
flashcards.back: Use PERMUTATIONS when ORDER MATTERS (arrangements, sequences). Use COMBINATIONS when ORDER DOESN'T MATTER (selections, subsets).
nPr = P(n,r) = n!/(n-r)!, representing the number of ways to arrange r objects selected from n distinct objects.
nCr = C(n,r) = n!/(r!(n-r)!), representing the number of ways to choose r objects from n distinct objects without regard to order.

flashcards.front: How many ways can n objects be arranged in a circle?
flashcards.back: (n-1)! ways. This is because we fix one object's position and arrange the remaining (n-1) objects relative to it.

flashcards.front: What is the formula for permutations with repeated objects?
flashcards.back: If n objects have repeats (n1 identical of type1, n2 of type2, ..., nk of typek), then total distinct permutations = n!/(n1! n2! ... nk!).
(x + y)^n = Σ C(n,r) x^(n-r) y^r for r=0 to n. The coefficients C(n,r) are called binomial coefficients.

flashcards.front: What is the complement method in counting?
flashcards.back: Instead of counting desired outcomes directly, count total outcomes minus outcomes we don't want: |A| = |S| - |A'|, where A' is the complement of A.

flashcards.front: How do you count arrangements with restrictions?
flashcards.back: First arrange the unrestricted items, then place restricted items in the gaps. Or use complementary counting if direct counting is complex.
Permutations where objects are not all distinct. If n objects include groups of identical objects, use n!/(n1!n2!...) to count unique arrangements.
